sections.paragraph:
to melt, to flow down, to waste away. In Kal once found, used of a sick person wasting away, Isa 10:18. (Kindred roots are מָסָה, מָאַס No. II; see also ماث to dissolve, to macerate in water, and the words given under מַי.)

Niphal נָמֵס, in pause נָמָס, fut. יִמַּס, inf. הִמֵּס-

(1) to be melted, used of the manna, Exo 16:21 of wax, Psal. 68:3 by hyperb. of mountains melting with blood, Isa 34:3.- Jud. 15:14 , “his bands melted from off his hands,” i.e. fell from his hands as if loosed. Used of cattle when sick, 1Sa 15:9.

(2) to become faint.-(a) with fear, terror, 2Sa 17:10 often used of the heart, Deu 20:8 Jos 2:11 5:1 . The original force of the expression is retained in the following, Jos 7:5, “the heart of the people melted, וַיְהִי לְמָֽיִם and became water.-(b) with sorrow, grief, Psa 22:15 112:10 compare Ovid. ex Ponto, i. 2, 57, “sic mea perpetuis liquescunt pectora curis, Ignibus admotis ut nova cera solet.”

Hiphil, causat. of Niphal No. 2, a, to make fearful, Deu 1:28.

Derivatives, מָס, תֶּמֶס; comp. also מַה, מִסָּה, which I refer however to another root.
